title: How You Can Use Few-Shot Learning In LLM Prompting - DZone
text: ### Few-shot learning helps guide AI models by showing them examples in your prompt. Few-shot learning sits in the middle, providing just enough guidance through examples without the complexity of full reasoning chains. ## **Few-Shot Examples That Work Well** A well-structured few-shot prompt contains clear sections and consistent formatting that makes the pattern easy for the model to detect. *Fig. 12: Example of a standard few-shot prompt, which might fail since there’s limited visibility into the thinking process* *Fig. 13: Example of a Chain-of-thought few-shot prompt to tackle complex questions* *Fig. 14: Example of a self-consistent few-shot prompt* *Fig. 15: Example of using prompts to instruct LLM to perform data cleaning*

title: Harness the Power of LLMs: Zero-shot and Few-shot Prompting
text: Zero-shot prompting is a specific scenario of zero-shot learning unique to Generative LLMs. In zero-shot, we provide no labeled data to the model and expect the model to work on a completely new problem. In the Twitter US Airlines Sentiment classification task, BERT fine-tuning was done with over 10,000 examples, whereas few-shot prompting needed only 20 to 50 examples to get similar performance. To show the power of few-shot prompting, we have compared the performance with SOTA and fine-tuned language models like BERT on tasks across standardized language understanding, translation, and QA benchmarks in the sections below. Few-shot achieves similar or better performance across classification, language understanding, translation, and question-answering tasks by providing labeled examples within prompts.

title: Fine-tuning large language models (LLMs) in 2025 - SuperAnnotate
text: LLM fine-tuning is the process of taking a pre-trained Large Language Model (LLM) and training it on a smaller, task-specific dataset to specialize it for a particular use case. Supervised fine-tuning is the process of updating a pre-trained language model with labeled data in order to perform a specific task. Best practices for fine-tuning involve clearly defining the target task, choosing the most suitable pre-trained model, preparing a high-quality, diverse, and relevant dataset for both training and evaluation, carefully adjusting hyperparameters, closely monitoring performance to avoid overfitting through techniques such as early stopping and regularization, exploring methods like LoRA for more efficient tuning, and validating results on a separate validation set to ensure the model generalizes effectively to new, unseen data.

title: A review on NLP zero-shot and few-shot learning - SpringerLink
text: Utilizing pre-trained language models for transfer learning emphasizes the robustness of contextual embedding in zero-shot scenarios, enabling models to understand subtle language patterns with minimal task-specific training [[24](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-025-07225-5#ref-CR24 "Ohkuma T, Nakayama H. By utilizing supplementary data, zero-shot learning allows models to generalize to classes that have not yet been seen, in contrast to classical supervised learning, which requires explicit labelling of every class [[25](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-025-07225-5#ref-CR25 "Bennequin E. Pre-trained Language Models: In a variety of natural language processing tasks, including zero-shot learning, models like BERT and GPT-3 are exhibiting outstanding performance, as shown in Table [3](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-025-07225-5#Tab3). For example, in few-shot image classification, a model trained on various datasets uses the principles of meta-learning to adjust to new classes with small sample sizes quickly [[46](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s42452-025-07225-5#ref-CR46 "Ma Y, Cambria E, Gao S.

title: Fine-tuning LLMs: overview and guide | Google Cloud
text: [Large language models](https://cloud.google.com/ai/llms) (LLMs) are powerful tools that can help with a lot of different tasks, from writing emails to answering complex questions. Fine-tuning and [Retrieval Augmented Generation](https://cloud.google.com/use-cases/retrieval-augmented-generation) (RAG) are two different ways to adjust LLMs for specific uses. *   **Improve accuracy on a particular task:** Fine-tuning can significantly improve the model's performance if you need it to do a specific task, like classifying customer reviews or generating product descriptions These pre-trained [models](https://cloud.google.com/discover/what-is-an-ai-model) have already learned a huge amount of general language knowledge from massive datasets. It can significantly improve the accuracy and relevance of the model's output for your specific use case, potentially reducing [AI hallucinations](https://cloud.google.com/discover/what-are-ai-hallucinations).

title: Zero-Shot, One-Shot, and Few-Shot Prompting: A Comparative Guide
text: Techniques like zero-shot, one-shot, and few-shot prompting allow models to generalize to new tasks or classes with little to no task-specific training examples. Zero-shot prompting is a technique in which an AI model is expected to solve a task without any examples provided for that specific task. One-shot prompting is a particular instance of few-shot learning where only a single example per new class or task is provided. Few-shot prompting involves providing the model with a small set (usually 2–5) of examples to clarify the task. Techniques such as N-way-K-shot classification illustrate this approach, where a model learns to classify data across multiple episodes of small tasks Zero-shot, one-shot, and few-shot prompting mark a transformative shift in how AI models approach learning from limited data.

title: LLM Training: Mastering the Art of Language Model Development
text: ## Large Language Model (LLM) training involves teaching AI models to understand and generate human-like text by processing vast amounts of data, significantly enhancing their language comprehension and production capabilities. Training an LLM, often referred to as pre-training, involves teaching the model to understand and generate language using a large, diverse dataset. title: LLM evaluation: Metrics, frameworks, and best practices - Wandb
text: ### Model-based evaluation metrics *   BERTScore: A metric that uses pre-trained language model embeddings (from BERT) to evaluate text similarity. The future likely holds integrated evals: e.g., an AI assistant that can see and talk might be evaluated on a scenario like "Describe what you see and answer questions about it while being factual and not biased." This requires combining NLP metrics with vision metrics. *   Community and Crowdsourced Evaluation: Projects like Dynabench have users trying to stump models, and when they succeed, that example is added to the test set. Some current eval tasks (like the Chain-of-Thought Prompting evaluation) informally do this; but it could be formalized, like a metric for logical consistency between explanation and answer, or a human rating of explanation helpfulness.
